feedback.text: Subject-matter: Feedback on the abusive practices of the video game industry relating to the shutdown of the games services purchased Ms, Mr, European institutions I am writing to you as a long-standing consumer of video games, in order to inform you of my deep disagreement with certain practices which today are common in the video industry and which seem to me to be both abusive, unethical and potentially illegal. It is becoming more and more frequent for games, which are bought at a high price by players, to be rendered completely inaccessible as a result of the closure of the servers or the closure of their online media. This trend, under the guise of a supposed “technological development” or unilateral business decisions, simply amounts to depriving the player of a good payment, without compensation or alternative. I recall here a fundamental principle of law: A purchase involves the durable possession of a good or service on the terms originally proposed. In many cases, however, the game purchased is not functional without connection to the servers (even solo), or becomes totally unusable once those servers are cut off. That situation transforms an act of purchase into a disguised rental, without any explicit mention or clear consent of the consumer having been established. I consider that logic to be a serious interference with users’ rights. It also contributes to the destruction of the video heritage, preventing the preservation, transmission or simply proofreading of important video works. As a player, but also as a citizen committed to a minimum level of contractual justice, it considers that: 1. Any game purchased must remain playable for life, at least in off-line version, or an equivalent solution (local servers, authorised Community patch, released source code) must be offered. 2. Service closures must be notified with clear notice, together with partial or full compensation for users. 3. Legislation should provide a firm framework for this type of drift, as is the case in other industries (books, films, music). I therefore invite you, as an actor or observer in this sector, to take a position on these abuses and to advocate for a return to sound logic: respect for the consumer, the sustainability of the work, and trust in the relationship between players and publishers. The European Union must be a tool for defending the fundamental rights of European citizens if it wants to win the hearts and minds of the population. Pending return, or concrete action to do so. Please accept, Sir, yours sincerely,
